summary: Gruber examines how Apple successfully kept its iPod lineup refresh and Intel processor switch secret from the rumor mill. He dismisses claims that Apple's lawsuits created a 'chilling effect' on rumor sites, arguing instead that Apple simply plugged its leaks effectively. He mocks Think Secret for retroactively claiming credit for predicting the iPod changes based on a vague, inaccurate earlier report. Most remarkably, the Intel switch — arguably the biggest Mac news in a decade — was kept almost entirely secret, with only Paul Thurrott catching wind of it as an aside at a Windows conference. Gruber graciously credits Thurrott for the scoop despite their adversarial history.
key_points: Apple's iPod refresh completely escaped the rumor mill, proving effective leak-plugging rather than any legal 'chilling effect'
Think Secret's attempt to claim prescience about the iPod changes is undercut by the vagueness and inaccuracy of their original prediction
The Intel switch was kept secret from the entire Mac community — developers, rumor sites, and commentators alike — until the Wall Street Journal broke it
Paul Thurrott, an unlikely source for Apple scoops, casually mentioned the Intel switch a month before it became public, likely from Intel-side sources
Gruber admits he was wrong about the Intel rumors and gives credit to Thurrott despite their long-running disagreements
money_quotes: Note to crackpots: this is not a "chilling effect" of Apple's lawsuits; to say so implies the rumor sites had information, but were intimidated from publishing it.
This is, simply, the result of good old-fashioned leak-plugging.
In Think Secret's world, this story qualifies as accurate.
This was quite probably the biggest Mac news in 10 years, and the Mac rumor sites had nothing.
Yes, the same Paul Thurrott whom I've long-berated as being always wrong regarding all things Apple. Except, this time, he was right. (And I was wrong.)
The entire Mac community was genuinely in the dark about this.
There were literally gasps at the WWDC keynote when Jobs unveiled the slide that said, "It's true."
But at least in some small circles that don't (or didn't) intersect with the Mac universe, it was an open secret.
key_insight: Apple's ability to keep major announcements secret was the result of disciplined leak-plugging, not legal intimidation, and the biggest scoops sometimes come from the most unexpected sources outside the usual echo chamber.
